author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm CV

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This Psalm is the first of those to which is prefixed the word Allelujah; the meaning of which word, or rather two words, is, Praise the Lord.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this reason he beginneth with praises: “O confess unto the Lord, and call upon His Name” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 1); for this confession is to be understood as praise, just as these words of our Lord, “I confess to Thee, O Father, Lord of heaven and earth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For after commencing with praise, calling upon God is wont to follow, whereunto he that prayeth doth next add his longings: whence the Lord’s Prayer itself hath at the commencement a very brief praise, in these words, “Our Father which art in Heaven.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The things prayed for, then follow.…This also followeth, “Tell the people what things He hath done;” or rather, to translate literally from the Greek, as other Latin copies too have it, “Preach the Gospel of His works among the Gentiles.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Unto whom is this addressed, save unto the Evangelists in prophecy?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “O sing unto Him, and play on instruments unto Him” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Praise Him both by word and deed; for we sing with the voice, while we play with an instrument, that is, with our hands.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let your talking be of all His wondrous works.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Be ye praise in His holy Name” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These two verses may without any absurdity seem paraphrases of the two words above; so that, “Let your talking be of all His wondrous works,” may express the words, “O sing unto Him;” and what followeth, “be ye praised in His holy Name,” may be referred to the words, “and play on instruments unto Him;” the former relating to the “good word” wherewith we sing unto Him, in which His wondrous works are told; the latter to the good work, in which sweet music is played unto Him, so that no man may wish to be praised for a good work on the score of his own power to do it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this reason, after saying, “be ye praised,” which assuredly they who work well deservedly may, he added, “in His holy Name,” since “he that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord.”…This is to be praised in His holy Name.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence we read also in another Psalm: “My soul shall be praised in the Lord: let the meek hear thereof, and be glad;” which here in a sense followeth, “Let the heart of them rejoice that seek the Lord:” for thus the meek are glad, who do not rival with a bitter jealousy those whom they imitate as already workers of good.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Seek the Lord, and be strengthened” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is very literally construed from the Greek, though it may seem not a Latin word: whence other copies have, “be ye confirmed;” others, “be ye corroborated.”…While these words, then, “Come unto Him, and be enlightened,” apply to seeing; those in the text relate to doing: “Seek the Lord, and be strengthened.”…But what meaneth, “Seek His face evermore”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I know indeed that to cling unto God is good for me; but if He is always being sought, when is He found?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Did he mean by “evermore,” the whole of the life we live here, whence we become conscious that we ought thus to seek, since even when found He is still to be sought?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To wit, faith hath already found Him, but hope still seeketh Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But love hath both found Him through faith, and seeketh to have Him by sight, where He will then be found so as to satisfy us, and no longer to need our search.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For unless faith discovered Him in this life, it would not be said, “Seek the Lord.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Also, if when discovered by faith, He were not still to be diligently sought, it would not be said, “For if we hope for that we see not, then do we with patience wait for it.”…And truly this is the sense of the words, “Seek His face evermore;” meaning that discovery should not terminate that seeking, by which love is testified, but with the increase of love the seeking of the discovered One should increase.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Remember,” he saith, “His marvellous works that He hath done, His wonders, and the judgments of His mouth” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This passage seemeth like that, “Thou shalt say unto the children of Israel, I Am hath sent me unto you:” an expression which, in ever so small part, scarce a mind taketh in.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Then mentioning His own Name, He mercifully mingled in His grace towards men, saying, “I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob; this is My Name for ever.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By which He would have it to be understood, that they whose God He declared Himself lived with Him for ever, and He said this, which might be understood even by children, that they who by the great powers of love knew how to seek His face for evermore, might according to their capacity comprehend, I Am that I Am.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Unto whom is it said, “O ye seed of Abraham His servant, ye children of Jacob, His chosen”? (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 6).…He next addeth, “He is the Lord our God: His judgments are in all the world” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is He the God of the Jews only?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He is the Lord our God:” because the Church, where His judgments are preached, is in all the world.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He hath been alway mindful of His covenant” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Other copies read, “for evermore;” and this arises from the ambiguity of the Greek.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if we are to understand “alway” of this world and not of eternity, why, when he explaineth what covenant He was mindful of, doth he add, “The word that He made to a thousand generations”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now this may be understood with a certain limitation; but he afterwards saith, “Even the covenant that He made with Abraham” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 9): “and the oath that He sware unto Isaac; and appointed the same unto Jacob for a law, and to Israel for an everlasting testament” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if in this passage the Old Testament is to be understood, on account of the land of Canaan; for thus the language of the Psalm runneth, “saying, Unto thee will I give the land of Canaan: the lot of your inheritance” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 11): how is it to be understood as everlasting, since that earthly inheritance could not be everlasting?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And for this reason it is called the Old Testament, because it is abolished by the New.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But a thousand generations do not seem to signify anything eternal, since they involve an end; and yet are also too numerous for this very temporal state.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For by howsoever few years a generation is limited, such as in Greek is called γεν™α, whereof the shortest period some have fixed is at fifteen years, after which period man hath the power of generation; what then are those “thousand generations,” not only from the time of Abraham, when that promise was made him, unto the New Testament, but from Adam himself down to the end of the world?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For who would dare to say that this world should last for 15000 years?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence it seemeth to me that we ought not to understand here the Old Testament, which it said through the prophet was to be cancelled by the New: “Behold, the days come, saith the Lord, when I will make a new covenant.”…After saying, “He hath been mindful of His covenant unto an age;” which we ought to understand as lasting for evermore, the covenant, namely, of justification and an eternal inheritance, which God hath promised to faith; he addeth, “and the Word that He commanded unto a thousand generations.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What meaneth “commanded”?…The command then was faith, that the righteous should live by faith; and an eternal inheritance is set before this faith.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “A thousand generations,” then, are, on account of the perfect number, to be understood for all; that is, as long as generation succeedeth generation, so long is it commanded to us to live by faith.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This the people of God doth observe, the sons of promise who succeed by birth, and depart by death, until every generation be finished; and this is signified by the number thousand; because the solid square of the number ten, ten times ten, and this taken ten times amounts to a thousand.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Even the covenant,” he saith, “which He made with Abraham: and the oath that He sware unto Isaac; and appointed the same unto Jacob,” that is, Jacob himself, “for a law.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These are the very three patriarchs, whose God He calleth Himself in a special sense, whom the Lord also doth name in the New Testament, where He saith, “Many shall come from the east and the west, and shall sit down with Abraham, and Isaac, and Jacob, in the kingdom of heaven.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is everlasting inheritance.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He next followeth out the history well known in the truth of the holy Scriptures.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “When they were in small numbers, very few, and they strangers in the land” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 12); that is, in the land of Canaan.…But some copies have the words “very few, and they strangers,” in the accusative case, the translator having turned the Greek phrase too literally into Latin.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If we were to render the whole clause in this way, we must say, “that they were very few, and they strangers;” but the phrase, “while they were,” is the meaning of the Greek; and the verb, “to be,” takes not an accusative, but a nominative after it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “What time as they went from one nation to another, from one kingdom to another people” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is a repetition of what he had said, “from one nation to another.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He suffered no man to do them harm: but reproved even kings for their sakes” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Touch not,” He said, “Mine anointed, and do My prophets no harm” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He declareth the words of God chiding or reproving kings, that they might not harm the holy fathers, while they were small in number, very few, and they strangers in the land of Canaan.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Although these words be not read in the books of that history, yet they are to be understood as either secretly spoken, as God speaketh in the hearts of men by unseen and true visions, or even as announced through an Angel.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For both the king of Gerar and the king of the Egyptians were warned from Heaven not to harm Abraham, and another king not to harm Isaac, and others not to harm Jacob; while they were very few, and strangers, before he went over into Egypt to sojourn with his sons: which is understood to be herein mentioned.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But since it occurred to ask, before they passed over and multiplied in Egypt, how so few in number, and those strangers in a foreign land, could maintain themselves: he next addeth, “He suffered no man to do them wrong,” etc.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But it may well excite a question, in what sense they were styled (Christs, or) anointed, before there was any unction, from which this title was given to the kings.…Whence then were those patriarchs at that time called “anointed”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For that they were prophets, we read concerning Abraham; and certainly, what is manifestly said of him, should be understood of them also.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Are they styled “christs,” because, even though secretly, yet they were already Christians?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For although the flesh of Christ came from them, nevertheless Christ came before them; for He thus answered the Jews, “Before Abraham was, I am.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But how could they not know Him, or not believe in Him; since they are called prophets for this very reason, because, though somewhat darkly, they announced the Lord beforehand?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence He saith Himself openly, “Your father Abraham desired to see My day, and he saw it, and was glad.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For no man was ever reconciled unto God outside of that faith which is in Christ Jesus, either before His Incarnation, or after: as it is most truly defined by the Apostle: “For there is one God, and one Mediator between God and men, the Man Christ Jesus.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He then beginneth to relate how it happened that they went from one nation to another, from one kingdom to another people.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He calleth,” he saith, “for a famine upon the land: and brake all the staff of bread” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thus it happened that they went from one nation to another, from one kingdom to another people.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But the expressions of the holy Scriptures are not to be negligently passed by.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He called,” he saith, “for a famine upon the land;” as if famine were some person, or some animated body, or some spirit that would obey Him who called.…Under this impression the old Romans consecrated some such deities, as the goddess Fever, and the god Paleness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or meaneth it, as is more credible, He said there should be famine; so that calling be the same thing as mentioning by name; mentioning by name, as speaking; speaking, as commanding?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Nor doth the Apostle say, “He calleth those things which be not, that they may be;” but, “as though they were.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For with God that hath already happened which, according to His disposition, is fixed for the future: for of Him it is elsewhere said, “He who made things to come.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And here when famine happened, then it is said to have been called, that is, that that which had been determined in His secret government, might be realized.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Lastly, he at once expounds, how He called for the famine, saying, “He brake all the staff of bread.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But He had sent a man before them” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Even Joseph.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How did He send him?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Joseph was sold to be a bond-servant.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When this happened, it was the sin of his brethren, and, nevertheless, God sent Joseph into Egypt.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We should therefore meditate on this important and necessary subject, how God useth well the evil works of men, as they on the other hand use ill the good works of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Next he doth relate the story, mentioning what Joseph suffered in his low estate, and how he was raised on high.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “His feet they hurt in the stocks: the iron entered into his soul, until his word came” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That Joseph was put in irons, we do not indeed read; but we ought no ways to doubt that it was so.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For some things might be passed over in that history, which nevertheless would not escape the Holy Spirit, who speaketh in these Psalms.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We understand by the iron which entered into his soul, the tribulation of stern necessity; for he did not say body, but “soul.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There is a somewhat similar expression in the Gospel, where Simeon saith unto Mary, “A sword shall pierce through thy own soul also.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That is, the Passion of the Lord, which was a fall unto many, and in which the secrets of many hearts were revealed, since their sentiments respecting the Lord were extorted from them, without doubt made His own Mother exceeding sorrowful, heavily struck with human bereavement.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now Joseph was in this tribulation, “until his word came,” with which he truly interpreted dreams: whence he was introduced to the king, that unto him also he might foretell what would happen in respect to his dreams.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But since he said, “Until his words were heard,” that we might not altogether so understand “his,” that any one might think so great an event was to be ascribed unto man; he at once added, “The word of the Lord inflamed him” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 19); or, as other copies have it more closely from the Greek, “The word of the Lord fired him,” that he also might be reputed amongst those to whom it is said, “Receive ye praise in His holy Name.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The king sent and loosed him, the prince of the peoples, and let him go free” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The “king” is the same as “the prince of the peoples:” he “loosed” him from his bonds “and let him go free” from his prison.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He made him lord also of his house: and ruler of all his substance” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “That he might inform his princes like unto himself, and teach his old men wisdom” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Greek hath, “and teach his elders wisdom.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which might altogether be rendered to the letter thus; “Might inform his princes like unto himself, and make his elders wise.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The word translated old men being presbyters or elders, not gerontas, old men: and to teach wisdom being from the Greek to sophize, which cannot be rendered by a single word in Latin, and is from the word sophia, wisdom, different from prudence, which is in Greek phronesis.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Yet we do not read this in the high elevation of Joseph, as we read not of fetters in his low estate.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But how could it happen that so great a man, the worshipper of the One True God, whilst in Egypt, should have been intent upon the nourishing of bodies, and the government of carnal matters only, and have felt no anxiety for souls, and how he could render them better?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But those things are written in that history, which, according to the intention of the writer, in whom was the Holy Spirit, were judged sufficient for signifying future events in that narration.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Joseph also came into Egypt, and Jacob was a stranger in the land of Ham” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Israel is the same with Jacob, as is Egypt with the land of Ham.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Here it is very plainly shown, that the Egyptian race sprang from the seed of Chain, the son of Noah, whose first-born was Canaan.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: So that in those copies wherein in this passage Canaan is read, we must alter the reading.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is better construed, “was a stranger,” than “dwelt,” as other copies have it: which would be the same as “was an inhabitant,” for it meaneth nothing different; the very same word is used in the Greek passage above, where it is said, “Very few, and they strangers in the land.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Moreover, the state of an incola or accola doth not signify a native, but a stranger.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Behold how “they went from one nation to another.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What had been briefly proposed, hath been briefly explained in the narration.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But from what kingdom they passed over to another people may well be asked.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For they were not yet reigning in the land of Canaan, because the kingdom of the people of Israel had not yet been established there.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How then can it be understood, except by anticipation, because the kingdom of their seed was destined there to exist?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Next is related what happened in Egypt.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And He increased,” he saith, “His people exceedingly, and made them stronger than their enemies” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Even the whole of this is briefly set forth, in order that the manner in which it took place may be afterwards related.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the people of God was not made stronger than their enemies the Egyptians, at the time when their male offspring were slain, or when they were worn out with making bricks; but when by His powerful hand, by the signs and portents of the Lord their God, they became objects of fear and of honour, until the opposition of the hardened king was overcome, and the Red Sea overwhelmed the persecutor with his army.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And He turned their heart so, that they hated His people, and dealt untruly with His servants” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is it to be in any wise understood or believed, that God turneth man’s heart to do sin?…For they were not good before they hated His people; but being malignant and ungodly, they were such as would readily envy their prosperous sojourners.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And so, in that He multiplied His own people, this bountiful act turned the wicked to envy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For envy is the hatred of another’s prosperity.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In this sense, therefore, He turned their heart, so that through envy they hated His people, and dealt untruly with His servants.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It was not then by making their hearts evil, but by doing good to His people, that He turned their hearts, that were evil of their own accord, to hatred.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For He did not pervert a righteous heart, but turned one perverted of its own accord to the hatred of His people, while He was to make a good use of that evil; not by making them evil, but by lavishing blessings upon those, which the wicked might most readily envy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The following verses, which are sung in praise of Him when Allelujah is chanted, show how He used this hatred of theirs, both for the trial of His own people, and for the glory of His Name, which is profitable for us.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He sent Moses His servant, and Aaron whom He had chosen him” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Whom He had chosen,” would be sufficient; but there is no difficulty in the addition of “him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is a phrase of Scripture, as, “The land in which they shall dwell in it:” a phrase which the divine pages are full of.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He set forth in them the words of His tokens, and of His wonders in the land of Ham” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We ought not to understand by “the words of His tokens,” words literally, words with which the tokens and wonders were worked, that is, which they uttered, that these tokens and wonders might take place.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For many were performed without words, either with a rod, or with outstretched hand, or by ashes sent towards heaven.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He sent darkness, and made it dark” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is also written among the plagues with which the Egyptians were smitten.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what followeth, is variously read in different copies.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For some have, “and they provoked His words;” while others read, “and they provoked not His words;” but the reading first mentioned we have found in most; while, where the negative particle is added, we could hardly discover two copies.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But perhaps the false reading has abounded owing to the easy sense; for what is easier understood than this, “They provoked His words,” that is, by their contumacious rebellions?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We have endeavoured to explain the other reading also according to some true sense: and this for the present occurs: “They provoked not His words,” that is, in Moses and Aaron; because they most patiently bore with a very stiffnecked people, until all things which God had determined to work by them, were fulfilled in order.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He turned their waters into blood, and slew their fish” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He made their land frogs, yea, even in the king’s chambers” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 30): as if he were to say, He turned their land into frogs.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there was so great a multitude of frogs, that this might well be said by hyperbole.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He spake the word, and there came all manner of flies, and lice in all their quarters” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If it be asked when He spake, it was in His Word before it took place; and there it was, without time, at what time it should take place: although even then He commanded it to be done, when it was to be done, through Angels, and through his servants Moses and Aaron.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He made their rains hail” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is a similar expression to the former, “He made their land frogs;” except that the whole land was not actually turned into frogs, though the whole of the rain may have been turned into hail.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “A burning fire in their land:” understand, “He sent.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He smote their vines also and fig-trees; and brake every tree of their coasts” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This was done by the violence of the hail, and by lightnings; whence he spoke of the fire as “burning.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He spake the word, and the locust came, and the caterpillar, of which there was no number” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The locusts and the caterpillars are one plague: of which the one is the parent, the other the offspring.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And did eat up all the grass in their land, and devoured the fruit of the ground” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Even grass is fruit, as Scripture is wont to speak, which calleth even the ripe corn grass; but it wished these two things to harmonize in number with the two which it had spoken of before, that is, the locust and the caterpillar.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But the whole of this doth belong to the variety of speech, which is a remedy for weariness, not to any difference of senses.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He smote every first-born in their land: even the first-fruits of all their strength” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is the last plague, excepting the death in the Red Sea.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The first-fruits of all their strength,” I imagine to be an expression derived from the first-born of cattle.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These plagues are ten in number, but they are not all mentioned, nor in the same order in which they are there read to have happened.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For praise-giving is free from the law which bindeth one who is relating or composing a history.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And since the Holy Spirit is the Author and Dictator, through the Prophet, of this praise; by the very same authority with which He guided him who wrote that history, he doth both mention something to have taken place which is not there read, and passeth over what is there read.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now he addeth this also to the praises of God, that He led the Israelites out of Egypt enriched with silver and gold; because even they were then in such a condition, that they could not as yet despise the just and due, though temporal, reward of their toils.…“He brought them forth also in silver and gold” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 37): this too is a Scripture idiom; for “in silver and gold” is said for the same as if it had been said “with silver and gold: there was not one feeble person among their tribes:” in body, not in mind.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This also was a great blessing of God, that in this necessity of removal there was no infirm person.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Egypt was glad at their departing: for their fear fell upon them” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 38); that is, the fear of the Hebrews upon the Egyptians.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For “their fear” is not that with which the Hebrews feared, but that with which they were feared.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Some one will say, how then were the Egyptians unwilling to dismiss them? why did they let them go as if they expected them to return? why did they lend them gold and silver, as to men who were to return, and to repay them, if “Egypt was glad at their departing”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But we must understand, after that final destruction of the Egyptians, and the terrible overthrow of the mighty pursuing army in the Red Sea, that the rest of the Egyptians feared lest the Hebrews should return, and with great ease crush the relics of them: illustrating what he had stated, that He made His people stronger than their enemies.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He now proceedeth to the divine blessings which were conferred upon them as they wandered in the desert.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He spread out a cloud to be their covering: and fire to give them light in the night season” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is as clear as it is well known.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “They asked, and the quail came” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: They did not desire quails, but flesh.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But since the quail is flesh, and in this Psalm he speaketh not of the provocation of those who did not please God, but of the faith of the elect, the true seed of Abraham; they are to be understood to have desired that that might come which might crush the murmurs of those who provoked.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Then in the next line, “And He filled them with the bread of heaven,” he has not indeed named manna, but it is obscure to none who hath read those records.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He opened the rock of stone, and the waters flowed out: so that rivers ran in the dry places” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This fact too is understood as soon as read.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But in all these blessings of His, God doth commend in Abraham the merit of faith.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the Psalmist goeth on to say, “For why?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He remembered His holy promise, which He made to Abraham His servant” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And He brought forth His people with joy, and His chosen with gladness” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What he said, “His people,” he has repeated in, “His chosen.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: So also what he said, “with joy,” he has repeated in, “with gladness.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And gave them the lands of the heathen: and they took the labours of the people in possession” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The lands of the heathen,” and “the labours of the people,” are the same; and the words, “He gave,” are repeated in these, “they took in possession.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 33. …“That they may keep His statutes, and seek out His law” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Lastly, since by the seed of Abraham he wished those to be understood here, who were truly the seed of Abraham, such as were not wanting even in that people; as the Apostle Paul clearly showeth, when he saith, “But not in all of them was God well pleased;” for if He was not pleased with all, surely there were some in whom He was well pleased: since then this Psalm praiseth such men as this, he hath said nothing here of the iniquities and provocations and bitterness of those with whom God was not well pleased.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But since not only the justice but also the mercy of Almighty God, the merciful, was shown even unto the wicked; concerning these attributes the rest of the Psalm pursueth the praises of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And yet both sorts were in one people: nor did the latter pollute the good with the contagion of their iniquities.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For “the Lord knoweth who are His;” and if he cannot separate in this world from wicked men, yet, “let every one that nameth the name of Christ depart from iniquity.”…
